## Content Craft — how a post earns engagement

The craft baseline **every draft starts from**, independent of brand. The brand kit
(`brand_voice`) carries *who* the brand is — tone, audience, banned words, hashtag
sets. This skill carries *how a post earns attention* — the same craft regardless of
brand. Read the brand kit first, then draft against these fundamentals; the platform
skills add the channel specifics (limits, native format) on top.

Don't wing each post. Work from the principles below, give it a real structure, and
make any fact followable. The bar: a reader scrolling past should have a reason to
stop, a payoff for stopping, and — if it's fact-bearing — a way to reach the source.

### 1. Engagement philosophy

Apply these to every post, across post types:

- **One idea per post.** A post that says one thing sharply beats one that says three
  things vaguely. A second idea is a second post.
- **Specific beats vague — always.** A number, a named outcome, a concrete detail
  out-pulls a mood. "Faster builds" → vague; "deploys went from 40 min to 90 sec" →
  concrete. Reach for the specific.
- **Open a gap early.** Curiosity, tension, or a stakes-y claim in the first line gives
  a reason to keep reading. A post that resolves itself in its first sentence has no pull.
- **Every line earns its place.** No throat-clearing ("We're excited to announce…"), no
  filler. If a line can be cut without losing meaning, cut it.
- **Design for saves and shares, not just likes.** The post that gets *saved* ("I'll
  need this later") or *sent to someone* ("this is so us") compounds over time. Utility,
  a sharp truth, or a reusable framework drives that — ask "would anyone save this?"
- **Native over link-dump.** Each platform rewards different shapes (links cost reach on
  X; IG captions aren't clickable). Format for the channel — the platform skill says how.
- **A CTA that invites action, not a reflex.** "Link in bio" is a reflex. A real CTA asks
  a question, invites a reply, or names the one next step. End on something a reader can *do*.

The brand kit decides the *voice* these are written in; the principles hold regardless of brand.

### 2. Layered copy structure

Build the post in layers — don't drop 1–3 facts and stop:

- **Hook** — the first line. Earns the stop: a specific claim, a tension, a surprising
  number, or a question that matters. (X: ~first 7 words; IG: first 125 chars before
  "…more"; FB: the opening sentence stands alone.)
- **Context / tension** — why it matters, what's at stake. The gap the hook opened, widened.
- **Payoff / insight** — the actual value: the lesson, the data, "here's what we found."
  This is what they came for — don't bury it or skip it.
- **CTA** — the one next step: reply with X, save this, read the source, try it.

Not every post spells out all four lines, but every post needs a **hook and a payoff** —
those two are non-negotiable. "Fact, fact, fact, #hashtag" is not a structure.

> **Flat (too basic):** "Anthropic released a new model. It's their newest. It's good at
> coding and reasoning."
> **Layered:** "The new model codes for a full workday without losing the thread. *(hook)*
> Most models drift after a few thousand tokens; this one holds context across long tasks.
> *(context)* On SWE-bench it lands [verified %], up from [prior %]. *(payoff)* Full
> benchmarks in the first comment — which task would you throw at it first? *(CTA + source
> + reply-invite)*"

(Fill `[verified %]` with a real, source-traced number — never an invented one. See §3.)

**By format:**
- **Single text post (X / Threads / Bluesky):** hook, one-line payoff, CTA. The limit forces tightness.
- **Caption (IG / FB):** hook in the visible preview, payoff in the body, CTA at the end; source via `first_comment`.
- **Carousel (IG):** the structure spreads across slides — see §5.
- **X thread:** tweet 1 = hook + the promise of what follows; each reply = one beat of the payoff; last tweet = CTA + link.

### 3. Accessible source attribution (every fact-bearing post)

If a post states a statistic, study, claim, or quote, the source must be **followable
from the post itself** — not just mentioned in chat.

This is distinct from primary-source *verification* (the `pipeline-orchestrator`'s rule:
confirm the number is *true* by tracing it to the original study). This is *attribution*:
making sure the reader can *reach* that source. Both are required — verify it's true, then
make it followable.

Reuse the channel each platform actually supports (no new plumbing):

- **X / Threads / Bluesky** — an inline link, or a link in a reply / last tweet (keeps the
  hook clean). Tag it with `link_tag` for attribution.
- **Instagram** — captions aren't clickable; put the source in `first_comment` ("Source:
  <name> — link in profile") or credit it on the image. A bare URL in the caption won't click.
- **Facebook** — a link in the caption with a line of context, or in `first_comment`.
- **On-image** — for a stat-driven graphic, put a small source line on the image itself
  (via `media_compose` `subtext` / footer) so the claim and its origin travel together.

Rule of thumb: **if the post makes a factual claim and a reader couldn't find where it came
from without asking you, it's not done.**

### 4. Hashtag intent

Hashtags are *discovery*, not decoration. Fewer and intentional beats a wall.

- Prefer niche/topic tags the right audience follows over broad high-volume tags (which bury you).
- **Per-platform counts live in the platform skills** (X: 0–2; IG: 3–8; …) and the
  **brand kit's hashtag sets** (`brand_voice`) — pull from those; don't invent a count here.
- Keep them out of a clean caption with `first_comment` (IG/FB) when the post reads better without them inline.

### 5. Carousels & multi-slide (Instagram)

A carousel is a narrative, not a photo dump. Structure the arc:

- **Slide 1 — the promise.** Earns the swipe: a hook plus a clear promise of the payoff
  ("5 mistakes that nearly killed our launch — #3 cost us a month"). No promise, no swipe.
- **Middle slides — one beat each.** One idea per slide, in sequence; each advances the
  payoff. Don't crowd two points on a slide — add a slide.
- **Final slide — CTA + source.** The one next step (save this, follow for more, read the
  source) plus any source credit.

Carousels are strong **save/share** drivers — make slide 1 promise something worth saving.
Render slides with the `output-manager` skill (one `media_compose` template per slide,
`image_urls[]` to publish), and keep one visual system across slides (same bg/accent/handle,
all from the brand kit).

### Where this fits

Read the brand kit (`brand_voice`) for *who* the brand is, then draft against these
fundamentals for *how the post earns attention*. The platform skills add channel specifics;
the **persona checklist enforces the non-negotiables** (real structure + followable sourcing)
before publish. For anything beyond a quick one-off, **offer the guided brief**
(`brief_schema`, via the `idea-input` / `research-trends` skills) up front rather than
jumping straight to a single draft.
